coredata.dc:title: Dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence method for malachite green detection based on multi-mechanism synergistic effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Zi-Bo
Zhang, Yan
Ren, Xing-Hui
He, Xi-Wen
Li, Wen-You
Zhang, Yu-Kui
coredata.dc:description: The proposition of ratiometric detection mode has demonstrated great superiority in improving analysis accuracy by forming self-calibration. Herein, the novel dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection for malachite green (MG) was first achieved based on synergistic effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). The ratiometric fluorescence probe (B-RCDs) was self-assembled via electrostatic attraction between blue-emission carbon dots (BCDs) and red-emission carbon dots (RCDs), followed with FRET effect from BCDs to RCDs and exhibited dual-emission at 450 nm and 627 nm. In the presence of MG, the IFE effect between MG and RCDs quenched the fluorescence at 627 nm and restored the fluorescence at 450 nm, sending out two reverse signals along with an obvious color change from pink to purple (302 nm UV lamp). This ratiometric method not only simplified the preparation process, but also improved the detection sensitivity, showing a low limit of detection (LOD) of 41.8 nM, which exhibited superiority than that of single-signal RCDs (157.3 nM). This method held a rapid response of 10 min and represented satisfactory recoveries (99.14%−109.08%) in real water samples, revealing it was a promising candidate in the fast, sensitive and practical detection of MG. Moreover, the design of synergistic effect supplied a new perspective for the development of ratiometric sensing in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric Fluorescence
Carbon Dots
Malachite Green Detection
Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer
Inner Filter Effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MG detection in real water samples
Establishment of B-RCDs sensing probe and mechanism discussion
Response mechanism of B-RCDs sensing probe to MG
Ratiometric detection of MG by B-RCDs sensing probe
Selectivity and anti-inference ability of B-RCDs sensing probe to MG
The practical application of B-RCDs for MG detection in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of BCDs (Insert: HRTEM image). (B) FT-IR spectrum of BCDs. (C) B 1 s XPS spectra of BCDs. (D) TEM image of RCDs (Insert: HRTEM image). (E) FT-IR spectrum of RCDs. (F) C 1 s XPS spectra of RCDs.

(A) The photo-emission spectra and corresponding photographs under UV lamp (insert image) of BCDs, RCDs, B-RCDs and B-RCDs + MG. Ex = 370 nm. (B) The photo-emission spectrum of BCDs (Ex = 370 nm) and UV-vis absorption spectrum of RCDs. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of BCDs, B-RCDs and B-RCDs + MG at 450 nm emission. (D) The photo-excitation, photo-emission spectra of RCDs and UV-vis absorption spectrum of MG. (E) Fluorescence decay curves of RCDs and RCDs + MG at 627 nm emission. (F) UV-vis absorption spectra of B-RCDs with different amounts of MG (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 μΜ). Insert image was an amplification of the 500–510 nm absorption spectra.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of B-RCDs with different amounts of MG (0, 0.2, 2, 4, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 and 50 μΜ). Conditions: B-RCDs, 15 μg/mL, V = 1.0 mL, pH = 6.0 (DSP-CA buffer, 0.02 M), Ex = 370 nm. (B) The plot of (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 versus MG concentration (0.2–50 μΜ) and the corresponding photographs under UV lamp. (C) CIE chromaticity diagram of B-RCDs with different concentrations of MG. (D) Fluorescence spectra of RCDs with different amounts of MG (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45 and 50 μΜ). Conditions: RCDs, 5 μg/mL, V = 1.0 mL, pH = 6.0 (DSP-CA buffer, 0.02 M), Ex = 540 nm. (E) The plot of ((Fr)0 – (Fr))/(Fr)0 versus MG concentration (1–50 μΜ) and the corresponding photographs under UV lamp. (F) CIE chromaticity diagram of RCDs with different amounts of MG.

(A) The value of (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 under different ions and biomolecules. (B) The value of (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 under different dye analogues in the absence and presence of MG. The concentrations of MG and analytes were 30 μM. Conditions: B-RCDs, 15 μg/mL, V = 1.0 mL, pH = 6.0 (DSP-CA buffer, 0.02 M), Ex = 370 nm.
Schematic diagram of construction method for B-RCDs probe and ratiometric response to MG.
Comparison with other fluorescence methods for MG detection.
Analytical results of B-RCDs to MG in tap water and lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence method for malachite green detection based on multi-mechanism synergistic effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Chromatographic Research and Analysis Center, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The proposition of ratiometric detection mode has demonstrated great superiority in improving analysis accuracy by forming self-calibration. Herein, the novel dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection for malachite green (MG) was first achieved based on synergistic effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). The ratiometric fluorescence probe (B-RCDs) was self-assembled via electrostatic attraction between blue-emission carbon dots (BCDs) and red-emission carbon dots (RCDs), followed with FRET effect from BCDs to RCDs and exhibited dual-emission at 450 nm and 627 nm. In the presence of MG, the IFE effect between MG and RCDs quenched the fluorescence at 627 nm and restored the fluorescence at 450 nm, sending out two reverse signals along with an obvious color change from pink to purple (302 nm UV lamp). This ratiometric method not only simplified the preparation process, but also improved the detection sensitivity, showing a low limit of detection (LOD) of 41.8 nM, which exhibited superiority than that of single-signal RCDs (157.3 nM). This method held a rapid response of 10 min and represented satisfactory recoveries (99.14%−109.08%) in real water samples, revealing it was a promising candidate in the fast, sensitive and practical detection of MG. Moreover, the design of synergistic effect supplied a new perspective for the development of ratiometric sensing in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) is a kind of triphenylmethane dye and displays widespread application in silk, leather and cotton [1,2]. Due to its unique antibacterial and insecticidal effect, MG is also utilized as insect repellent, bactericide and preservative [3-5]. However, MG converts to high-toxicity leuco-malachite green (LMG) once entering the organism, which has many adverse effects on human body and causes water pollution [6,7]. Nowadays, MG has been forbidden in the aquatic and food industries in many countries [8]. There are many methods developed for MG detection so far, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods [9], HPLC-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) methods [10], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) methods [11] and immunological methods [12]. However, HPLC-based technology needs complex sample pretreatment and has a long detection time cycle, which is only carried out in the laboratory [13,14]. The metal substrate used in SERS method is usually based on Au or Ag and can not be reused, which increases the production cost [4]. Immunological method has strong specificity because of the specific combination of antigen and antibody, but it requires the synthesis of artificial antigens and is prone to false positives [15,16]. As we all know, fluorescent methods have the advantages of fast speed, low cost, no pretreatment and simple instrument [17,18]. Fluorescent method can provide true information about the location and number of targets through interaction between analytes and probes [19]. In addition, fluorescence method allows analysis visually by intuitive color changes under the ultraviolet light, thus has developed as one of the prevalent analytical methods.

The sensing mechanisms in fluorescence methods mainly include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). FRET refers to that the fluorescence emission of the donor excites the acceptor fluorescence and results in the quenching of the donor fluorescence [20]. The condition is that the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the acceptor, and the fluorescent molecules are close enough (less than 10 nm), which belongs to dynamic quenching. IFE refers to that the analyte absorbs energy at the emission wavelength and excitation wavelength of fluorescent molecules and causes fluorescence quenching [21]. The condition is that the absorption spectrum of the analyte overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorescent molecule, which belongs to static quenching. Up to now, many fluorescence materials have been reported to be effective in detecting MG based on FRET or IFE, such as CdTe quantum dots [22], lanthanide-chelated metal-organic frameworks [23], fluorescein [24] and carbon dots [25]. However, due to the lack of self-calibration, traditional fluorescent methods for MG determination exhibit single-signal (“turn on” or “turn-off”), which hold susceptibility to many factors, such as the excitation light fluctuation, sample matrix and detection environment [26,27]. Herein, introducing intrinsic correction to interferences from background signals and detection systems is highly necessary.

To solve the problem, ratiometric analysis mode is proposed to minimize these external influences and accelerate detection accuracy [28]. To construct ratiometric fluorescence probes, one scheme is to bring in a target-insensitive fluorophore as reference apart from response section [29-31], another is to incorporate two simultaneous response fluorophores into one probe [32-34]. Usually, the ratio of two fluorescence peaks is used as the response signal when constructing the regression equation in the ratiometric fluorescence methods. Therefore, compared with ratiometric probes with one reference signal, ratiometric probes with two reverse signal changes (dual-reverse-signal mode) possess greater signal value variation, resulting in a greater signal-to-noise ratio and higher sensitivity [35]. At the same time, in virtue of the simultaneous reverse responses of two different-emission peaks (one enhanced and another weakened), even tiny signal fluctuation can cause significant color change, which is easier to be distinguished by naked eyes to realize visualization detection [36]. Given the notable superiority, the construction of dual-reverse-signal response probes deserves thorough exploration.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) own unique features of stable optical properties, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and facile preparation processes [37-39]. In recent years, CDs have acted as a fluorophore to participate in the construction of ratiometric fluorescence probes together with other materials (such as quantum dots [40], metal nanoclusters [41] and upconversion nanoparticles [42]) to detect ions, small molecules, biomarkers, and so forth [29,43-45]. However, CDs only served as reference signal in these methods and did not participate in the response to the target. Nowadays, ratiometric fluorescence probes using CDs as two response signals are rarely reported and the design strategy of the CDs-based ratiometric nanoprobe still have much room to improve.

Herein, a facile self-assembly strategy was proposed for the construction of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe and the dual-reverse-signal response to MG was achieved for the first time. As presented in Scheme 1 , blue-emission CDs (BCDs) and red-emission CDs (RCDs) were synthesized through solvothermal methods, respectively. Then the sensing probe B-RCDs was self-assembled via the electrostatic combination between BCDs and RCDs, which was simple and fast. Due to the FRET from BCDs to RCDs, the fluorescence of BCDs was quenched and B-RCDs exhibited dual-emission at 450 and 627 nm. MG addition caused decreased fluorescence of RCDs via IFE, which weakened FRET inside probe and restored the fluorescence of BCDs. The simultaneous variation of fluorescence signals led to a successive color change from pink to purple and showed the potential of visualization detection. Finally, the proposed analytical method was employed in the residue monitor of MG in real water samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first establishment of dual-reverse-signal detection mode for MG, which greatly improved the analysis accuracy and detection sensitivity. Both of two different-emission CDs served as response signals, improving the potential of the usage of CDs as sensing agent in dyes residue monitoring. Taking advantage of synergistic effect based on the interaction between FRET and IFE, this strategy also widened train of thoughts in the prospective development of multi-signal sensing.

3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid (APBA) and sodium citrate was obtained from J&K Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Urea was obtained from TCI chemicals (Tianjin, China). Citric acid and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from HEOWNS Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Aladdin BioChem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was supplied by Concord Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Malachite green was purchased from Guangfu Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All the reagents used were of analytical reagent grade. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was used in the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were observed on F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on FLS-920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). UV-vis absorption studies were explored by using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology investigation was performed on JEM-2800 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected by Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (Japan). The compositions of prepared materials were investigated by TENSOR II FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, German) and Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (UK). Hydrated diameter and zeta potential were obtained through Zetasizer Nano ZS dynamic light scattering meter (UK).

The blue-emitting BCDs was obtained via a one-step hydrothermal method with some modifications [46]. APBA (0.1 g) was added into ultrapure water (10 mL) and sonicated for 10 min. Then, 0.1 M NaOH (0.2 mL) was added to obtain transparent yellow solution. Afterwards, the mixture was heated to 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the precipitate was abandoned by centrifugation and the supernatant was dialyzed (MWCO 500, 12 h) to remove unreacted materials. Last, the solid-state BCDs were collected by freeze-drying.

The red-emitting RCDs was obtained based on a reported method [47]. Urea (2.0 g) and CA (1.0 g) were added into DMF (10 mL) and sonicated for dissolution. Then the mixture was heated to 180 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling, the obtained solution (2 mL) was mixed with NaOH solution (4 mL, 50 mg/mL) and centrifuged to remove supernatant. Then the dark brown precipitation was washed by water for three times. Last, the solid-state RCDs were collected by freeze-drying.

The B-RCDs sensing probe was prepared by self-assembly of two CDs. Briefly, RCDs solution (5 mL, 0.1 mg/mL) and BCDs solution (5 mL, 0.2 mg/mL) were mixed together and incubation for 5 min to obtain B-RCDs solution (stock solution). For later measurement, the stock solution was diluted by disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid (DSP-CA) buffer solution (0.02 M, pH 6.0).

B-RCDs stock solution (100 μL) was added into MG solution with different concentrations and diluted to 1 mL by DSP-CA buffer solution (0.02 M, pH 6.0). After 10 min incubation, the fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded under 370 nm excitation wavelength. To explore the selectivity of the B-RCDs sensing probe, MG was substituted by other ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Al3+, NH4 +, SO4 2-, NO3 –, Cl-, CH3COO–), small molecules (fructose, glucose, alanine, serine, glutathione, glycine, urea) and other interfering dyes (CV, MB, AR, TA, BF, AO, CR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the established probe, tap water and lake water were chosen as the real samples. The tap water sample was obtained from residential water and the lake water was collected from Xinkai Lake in Nankai University. The water samples were treated by centrifugation (13,000 rpm, 10 min) and filtration (0.22 μM membrane, 3 times) to remove large impurities. Then B-RCDs stock solution (100 μL) and water samples (100 μL) were added to different concentrations of MG solution and diluted to 1 mL by DSP-CA buffer solution (0.02 M, pH 6.0). Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the system were tested under 370 nm excitation after 10 min incubation.

The morphological structure of prepared BCDs was characterized by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, BCDs were mono-dispersive and exhibited a well-resolved (100) lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which consisted with the peak at 42° in XRD pattern (Fig. S1A) and proved the successful preparation. The average diameter of BCDs was 2.45 nm based on the 200 individual particals (Fig. S1B). BCDs showed a blue emission of 450 nm under 370 nm excitation and a characteristic absorption peak at 297 nm (Fig. S1C), which was attributed to π → π* transition of C=C bond [48]. Functional groups were described by FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, the absorption peaks at 3326 and 1581 cm−1 were attributed to the N-H stretching vibrations and bending vibrations, respectively. The B-O stretching vibration was located on the absorption peak at 1060 cm−1, and the absorption peaks at 1099 and 1442 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-N and -C-N=, respectively. XPS spectra were implemented to further investigated the surface composition. Fig. S1D showed BCDs contained four elements of C, N, B and O. The high-resolution spectra of B 1 s (Fig. 1C) demonstrated two peaks at 190.3 eV and 191.4 eV, which were assigned to B-C and B-O. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. S1E), three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, and 283.9 eV were attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and C-B. As shown in Fig. S1F, N 1 s spectra were decomposed into three fitted peaks at 399.0 eV (pyridinic N), 399.7 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.4 eV (graphitic N), respectively. Above results proved that B and N were successfully doped in the BCDs and the surface of the BCDs was rich in amino and boronic acid groups.

The characterization of RCDs was illustrated in Fig. 1D. RCDs exhibited good dispersion and showed a well-resolved (002) lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, which was in conformity with the peak at 26° of XRD pattern (Fig. S2A). The sizes of RCDs distributed from 3.05 to 4.45 nm and the average diameter was about 3.85 nm (Fig. S2B). Fig. S2C showed RCDs possessed excitation-independent property under the excitation wavelength between 460 and 580 nm. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1E), the broad absorption band at the region of 3116–3655 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H. The strong stretching vibration absorption peak at 1580 cm−1 proved the existence of COO–, which repressed the non-radiative recombination process on the surface of RCDs and enhanced the fluorescence quantum yield [49,50]. Full XPS spectrum (Fig. S2D) explained that RCDs was composed of C, N and O. As demonstrated in Fig. 1F, C 1 s spectra were decomposed into four peaks, which were attributed to C-C/C=C (284.6 eV), C-N/C-O (285.4 eV), C-C=O (287.4 eV) and C=O (288.1 eV), respectively. Above results proved that the surface of RCDs was abundant with carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of B-RCDs sensing probe and mechanism discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A showed the fluorescence spectra of BCDs, RCDs and B-RCDs under 370 nm excitation. As mentioned above, BCDs and RCDs exhibited emission peak at 450 nm and 627 nm, respectively, which corresponded to blue fluorescence and orange-red fluorescence under UV lamp (λex = 302 nm). After mixed together, the fluorescence of BCDs was significantly quenched by RCDs and the hybrid B-RCDs exhibited dual-emission at 450 and 627 nm along with a mixed pink color. As illustrated in Fig. S3A, with the increase of RCDs concentration from 0 to 9 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of B-RCDs at 450 nm descended dramatically while that at 627 nm increased subsequently. Insert image showed that under different amounts of RCDs, the B-RCDs displayed gradual color alteration from blue to pink under 302 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. S3B, there exhibited a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.995) between the ratio of Fr/Fb and RCDs concentrations (Fr referred to the fluorescence intensity at 627 nm and Fb stood for the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm). This result could be explained by the FRET model, where BCDs acted as a donor and RCDs as an acceptor. According to the previous characterization results, the surface of BCDs was electropositive due to the abundant amino and boronic acid groups, while that of RCDs was electronegative because of the ample carboxyl groups. Therefore, it was accessible for the two CDs to combine with each other through electrostatic interaction, following with FRET occurred.

To confirm this hypothesis, the spectral properties of two components were investigated and there found an overlap between the emission spectrum of BCDs and the absorption spectrum of RCDs (Fig. 2B), which was a premise of FRET. Zeta potential and DLS measurements were performed, successively. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the zeta potential of BCDs, RCDs and B-RCDs were 10.5, −44.7 and −35.3 mV, respectively, and the hydrated diameter of B-RCDs was significantly larger (17.1 nm) than that of BCDs (6.4 nm) or RCDs (7.4 nm) (Fig. S5), which proved the combination of two CDs and successful assembly of B-RCDs. As is known to all, FRET usually occurred through long-range dipole-dipole interactions over distances about 1–10 nm to ensure the energy transfer [20]. Therefore, the close proximity of BCDs to RCDs made it more conducive for the proceeding. Fluorescence lifetime was also measured to verify the mechanism. Fig. 2C showed the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs decreased from 5.53 to 3.85 ns after the combination with RCDs, which suggested a dynamic quenching process and further confirmed the possibility of FRET mechanism.

Then the stability of B-RCDs was explored. As demonstrated in Fig. S6, whether in long-time irradiation of 370 nm (60 min) or high concentration of NaCl solution incubation (4 M), the fluorescence intensity of B-RCDs kept constant in the process, which revealed good photostability and ionic strength stability of the synthesized probe as well as confirmed the reliability of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of B-RCDs sensing probe to MG
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of B-RCDs in MG detection, 50 μM of MG was added into B-RCDs solution to observe the fluorescence change. Fig. 2A showed the emission intensity of B-RCDs at 627 nm decreased sharply while that at 450 nm restored, sending out two reverse signals. In the meanwhile, B-RCDs displayed an obvious color variation from pink to purple under UV lamp. The probe showed the potential of application in dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection of MG.

Then the mechanism in the detecting process was explored. Fig. S7A revealed that MG caused negligible influence on the fluorescence of BCDs. The intensity of blue emission changed slightly with MG addition and that was proved by CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. S7B. In contrast, the addition of MG to RCDs gave rise to a significant weakening on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S8). Accordingly, it was RCDs that responded to MG in the ratiometric probe. Fig. 2D displayed the emission spectrum of RCDs was widely overlapped with the absorption spectrum of MG, suggesting there existed FRET or IFE process. The fluorescence lifetimes of RCDs anterior and posterior to MG addition were 1.07 and 1.01 ns, respectively (Fig. 2E), which showed the response between RCDs and MG was a static quenching process caused by IFE effect.

In addition, the UV-vis absorption spectra in the detecting process were investigated in Fig. 2F and Fig. S9. Along with the addition of MG, the intensity of MG characteristic absorption peak at 627 nm gradually increased, while the absorption peak of B-RCDs at 505 nm gradually reduced and finally disappeared. On the basis of previous characterization, BCDs had no absorption at 505 nm (Fig. S1C) but RCDs owned a similar absorption band at the same position (Fig. 2B). As a result, it was speculated that the disappearance of the peak was attributed to the interaction between RCDs and MG, which depressed the FRET effect between BCDs and RCDs and restored the fluorescence of BCDs. The fluorescence lifetime of B-RCDs at 450 nm increased from 3.85 to 4.31 ns after MG addition (Fig. 2C), also confirmed the fluorescence recovery of BCDs. In summary, due to the synergistic effect of FRET and target-induced IFE, the proposed B-RCDs probe exhibited two reverse signals to MG of “turn-off” and “turn-off-on”, which presented ratiometric response mode and eliminated external influences. The design of this method not only improved the detection accuracy, but also provided new ideas for the construction of ratiometric probes based on the introduction of synergistic effect.

As illustrated in Fig. S10, B-RCDs exhibited different degrees of dual-reverse-signal response to MG under four mass ratios of BCDs/RCDs. Observed under UV lamp, the chromaticity difference during detection changed most obviously in the mass ratio of 10:5. For quantitative comparison, the normalized fluorescence intensity ratio of (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 was employed to evaluate the response effects ((Fb/Fr)0 referred to the fluorescence intensity ratio of BCDs to RCDs without MG. Fig. S11A indicated that 10:5 (BCDs/RCDs) was determined to be the optimized mass ratio.

Fig. S11B showed the value of (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 reached the maximum in the pH 6.0. When the pH value was below 6.0, the decrease of response efficiency probably resulted from the protonation of carboxy in acid conditions, which induced non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs on the surface of RCDs and quenched RCDs fluorescence [51], followed with depressed energy transfer efficiency. Fig. S12A demonstrated that the optical property of B-RCDs was greatly affected in the over acid or over alkali environment. Then with the increase of pH value, the deprotonation of amino group on the surface of BCDs greatly influenced the interaction between BCDs and RCDs, and significantly reduced the FRET efficiency. In addition, as a cationic dye, MG was unstable in alkaline conditions. UV-vis absorption spectra in Fig. S12B revealed the decrease of MG contents in high pH environment. Therefore, when the pH value was above 6.0, the declined activity of MG also reduced the response efficiency.

The excitation conditions and equilibrium time were also optimized. Fig. S13 showed that the two emission peaks of B-RCDs were excitation independent under excitation of 350–390 nm. Curves in Fig. S14A showed that (Fb/Fr)/(Fb/Fr)0 reached the maximum under excitation wavelength of 370 nm. This may be because 370 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength for BCDs, which was more favorable for the FRET process from BCDs to RCDs. Fig. S14B showed that the fluorescence intensity of probe tended to stable after 10 min after MG addition, revealing a rapid response speed. Therefore, subsequent experiments were performed in DSP-CA buffer solution (pH 6.0, 0.02 M) under 370 nm excitation within 10 min incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of MG by B-RCDs sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the superiority of this ratiometric probe, the single-signal response of RCDs to MG was explored as a comparison. Fig. 3D and Fig. 3E showed the addition of MG induced decreased fluorescence intensity of RCDs. However, due to the lack of blue emission of BCDs, the color tonality change under UV lamp was tedious and the coordinates in CIE chromaticity showed a narrower moving range from (0.4932, 0.2787) to (0.4202, 0.2738), which was relatively difficult to distinguish (Fig. 3F). The quenching efficiency of ((Fr)0 – Fr)/(Fr)0 displayed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) with MG concentrations from 1 to 50 μM, and the regression equation was presented as: ((Fr)0 – Fr)/(Fr)0 = 0.013c – 0.019 ((Fr)0 referred to the fluorescence of RCDs without MG). The LOD of this single-signal detection method was calculated to be 157.3 nM, which was 2 times larger than that of B-RCDs and illustrated the design of dual-reverse-signal response mode greatly improved the detection sensitivity. Proved by comparison, the design of ratiometric method greatly improved the detection sensitivity. In addition, because the human eye is more sensitive to the chromaticity change than brightness change, the dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence method expanded visual chromatism and was more conducive to visualization analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-inference ability of B-RCDs sensing probe to MG
ce:section.ce:para: Some coexisting foreign substances and dye analogues were selected to explore the selectivity and anti-interference ability of B-RCDs. Fig. 4 A showed the addition of ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, SO4 2-, NO3 –, Cl- and CH3COO–) or biomolecules (fructose, glucose, alanine, serine, glutathione, glycine and urea) induced negligible effect on the fluorescence of B-RCDs. In Fig. 4B, the dyes chosen included crystal violet (CV), methyl blue (MB), alizarin red (AR), tartrazine (TA), basic fuchsin (BF), acid orange (AO) and congo red (CR). The result indicated that B-RCDs was selective to MG and the presence of other dyes had little influence on the determination of MG, demonstrating that the proposed probe was an ideal candidate for MG detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practical application of B-RCDs for MG detection in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its excellent analysis performance, the practical application of B-RCDs probe to MG was explored in tap water and lake water. After measurement, no MG was detected in water sample. Then spiked and recovery method was adopted in the following experiments. As shown in Table 2 , satisfactory recoveries were obtained from 99.14% to 109.08% with relative standard deviation (RSD) below 5.73%. Therefore, B-RCDs exhibited outstanding capability in practical application.

In summary, a noval ratiometric fluorescence probe was prepared based on CDs through a facile self-assembly strategy, and the dual-reverse-signal response for MG was achieved for the first time. This method held many prominent advantages as follows. Firstly, the probe was constructed via electrostatic interaction between BCDs and RCDs, which avoided complicated synthesis and simplified the preparation process. Secondly, both BCDs and RCDs acted as response sections to produce simultaneous reverse signals, which improved the analysis accuracy and sensitivity to a large degree. Thirdly, the introducing of synergistic effect of multi mechanism provided ideas for the prospective design of multi-signal sensing assay. Lastly, the variation of two fluorescence peaks resulted in an obvious color variation, which could be captured by naked eyes and exhibited great potential in visualization detection. This work not only provides a method for the rapid and sensitive detection of MG, but also exhibits the potential of applying dual-reverse-signal mode for dyes residue monitoring in the future.

Zi-Bo Ma: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yan Zhang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xing-Hui Ren: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xi-Wen He: Writing – review & editing. Wen-You Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yu-Kui Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1602400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121196.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
